name: 350.org
mission: 350.org is building a global grassroots climate movement that can hold our leaders accountable to the realities of science and the principles of justice. That movement is rising from the bottom up all over the world, and is uniting to create the solutions that will ensure a better future for all. Our online campaigns, grassroots organizing, and mass public actions bring together a global network active in over 188 countries
theoryOfChange: 350.org believes that by building a global grassroots movement (people power), they can dismantle the influence of the fossil fuel industry (resistance) and accelerate the transition to community-led renewable energy (solutions).

dimension_explanations.alignment: The organization offers a medium fit for donors, as its work focuses on high-urgency climate advocacy [5]. While it is a sadaqah-eligible cause, its alignment score of 22/50 reflects a focus on systemic policy change rather than traditional direct-aid models [4].
dimension_explanations.impact: 350.org demonstrates high impact through its efficient use of capital, maintaining 7.2 months of working capital and directing 81.5% of its expenses toward programs [4][5]. Its direct service in training and mobilization provides a clear pathway for climate action [1].

baseline_narrative.headline: 350.org is a high-impact climate advocacy organization dedicated to mobilizing a global movement to challenge the systems driving the climate crisis.
strengths: High program expense ratio of 81.5% ensuring funds reach core missions
Strong fundraising efficiency of $0.12 per $1 raised
baseline_narrative.summary: 350.org focuses on training and mobilizing a diverse climate movement to implement solutions and lead US-based campaigning efforts [1][4]. The organization maintains a strong financial standing with a total revenue of $19,507,313 and a high level of accountability [4][5].

rich_narrative.headline: 350.org is a high-impact climate advocacy organization dedicated to mobilizing a global movement to challenge the systems driving the climate crisis.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who prioritize systemic change over direct aid and want to support high-accountability climate advocacy through sadaqah.
donor_motivations: Want to address the root causes of the climate crisis
Value high transparency and third-party ratings
Prefer supporting grassroots mobilization and training
Interested in fossil fuel divestment as a lever for change
giving_considerations: Donations support advocacy which has long-term, non-linear outcomes
The organization is currently experiencing a revenue contraction
Most funds are spent on US-based operations and staff
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking to fulfill zakat obligations or those looking for immediate, tangible humanitarian relief like food or medicine.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: The organization has a strong theory of change and verified outcomes, though it lacks RCT-level evidence common in advocacy.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: 350.org creates impact by training local leaders to run campaigns that pressure financial institutions and governments to move away from fossil fuels.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence in advocacy ensures that mobilization efforts are actually shifting policy and capital.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: The organization is facing a <cite id="25">-8.0% revenue CAGR</cite>, suggesting a significant need for new funding to maintain its global campaign scale.

organizational_capacity.geographic_reach: Global operations with focus on 10+ countries

peer_comparison.differentiator: Distinguished by its <cite id="6">81.5% program ratio</cite>, which is highly competitive compared to the <cite id="10">peer median of 90.3%</cite>.

similar_organizations.differentiator: More focus on scientific research and technical analysis

strengths.detail: The organization holds an <cite id="9">overall score of 99.99.66666666666667/100 from Charity Navigator</cite>, reflecting near-perfect marks in governance and financial health.
With a <cite id="6">program expense ratio of 81.5%</cite>, 350.org significantly exceeds the <cite id="10">industry standard of 75%</cite> for non-profit efficiency.
The organization maintains a <cite id="11">verified theory of change</cite> and has earned a <cite id="12">Gold Seal of Transparency from Candid</cite> for its outcome reporting.

strengths_deep_dive: 350.org demonstrates exceptional financial resilience, maintaining <cite id="13">7.2 months of working capital</cite> to sustain operations during economic fluctuations. This stability is paired with rigorous internal oversight, including a <cite id="14">board of 12 members</cite> where <cite id="15">100% of members are independent</cite>. Their impact is amplified through strategic grantmaking, distributing <cite id="16">$1,860,387 in total grants</cite> to local partners, including <cite id="17">$1,029,826 to foreign recipients</cite> to bolster climate leadership in vulnerable regions.
rich_narrative.summary: Founded in <cite id="1">2008</cite>, 350.org has emerged as a central pillar in the global climate movement, focusing on <cite id="2">training and mobilizing a diverse grassroots network</cite> to challenge fossil fuel systems. The organization operates with significant scale, reporting <cite id="3">$19,587,115 in total revenue</cite> for the 2023 tax year. Its strategy centers on <cite id="4">fossil fuel divestment, climate finance advocacy, and renewable energy solutions</cite> across a geographic reach that includes <cite id="5">over 10 countries</cite> such as Brazil, Egypt, and Australia. Financially, the organization is highly efficient, maintaining a <cite id="6">program expense ratio of 81.5%</cite>, which ensures the vast majority of donor funds are directed toward core advocacy and mobilization efforts rather than administrative overhead. This efficiency is further evidenced by a <cite id="7">fundraising efficiency of $0.12 per $1 raised</cite>, well below industry averages for advocacy groups. Despite its international mission, the organization maintains a <cite id="8">strong domestic presence in the United States</cite>, where it funds local affiliates to drive regional climate policy.
